Transcrição Completa: 65 Building Out The 6 Emails Per Day Hammer Them Sequence 12 7 24
[00:00] All right. So, we had a request uh earlier this week. I believe it was from Dicki if I'm not mistaken on the hammer them strategy, specifically the email component. So, I kind of realized that I hadn't gone through with I haven't gone through that specifically with you guys yet. So, I wanted to take some time to do kind of like a walkthrough exercise together of actually doing it.
[00:25] Uh rather than just teaching it to you, I figured we could actually do it collectively here today. Allow me to first just explain uh how it works from like a strategy perspective. So we make sure we get that part covered and then uh we'll dive into the exercise.
[00:42] So remember the hammer them strategy's intention okay the intention of the campaign is to turn the sales people into cashiers and in order to do that we have to essentially take all the information that would otherwise be asked, talked about and considered inside of a sales call and put it all in front of the person prior to the sales call.
[01:08] We do that mainly through the content and we ideally with the content have anywhere between 30 and 50 pieces of content collectively just hammering the shit out of people pre-call or pre-event such as as an example like pre-webinar and the goal is to have a minimum frequency of at least 15 to 20.
[01:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Hammer Them Strategy - Email Component"
NÚMEROS: 30-50 pieces of content, 15-20 minimum frequency, 6 emails per day, 18 total emails, 72-hour delay
VISUAL: Estrutura visual mostrando fluxo de emails, timeline de 72 horas, frequência de distribuição de conteúdo
[01:35] Okay. Now when it comes to the email side of the hammer them strategy we want a frequency count of at least 18. The hammer them strategy email component sends upwards of six emails a day.
[01:49] Now again, you could always stretch that out if your timeline is greater. Uh keep in mind the six emails a day is the standard that I set it at because in most instances when you run it with a book a call funnel, you have about a 72-hour delay prior to the call generally occurring.
[02:09] Um, there's a few little nuanced questions I just want to get out of the way real fast, which is, as an example, like, what happens if the call happens in 48 hours or 24 hours? Well, in that case, the person just gets far less emails. You don't compress the full 18 emails to send off prior to the call.
[02:28] You ideally just set it off to where there's about six emails a day between the 12 hours that you decide to set it to. As an example, it could be like 8:00 a.m. through 8:00 p.m. that you send your six emails every other hour.
[02:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Dynamic Marketing Automation Setup"
NÚMEROS: 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM window, 6 emails, every 2 hours
VISUAL: Timeline visual mostrando distribuição de emails ao longo do dia, sistema de tags, automação de CRM
[02:48] as an example, um you are doing dynamic marketing automation in order to stop the email sequence once somebody's call actually happens. So, inside of a CRM, you could typically either create a specific field for it or you're just using a field that already exists or you're using a tag to signify through marketing automation.
[03:10] You could use Zapier for this as an example. Um, okay. So a person booked a call, a tag gets applied that starts this specific marketing automation sequence of emails and then in addition to that you can have a zap that says apply this tag that the call was a no-show, cancelled or did happen, which again would then pull them out of that specific sequence.
[03:35] Um, you could do it with a tag like what I just articulated, which I think is the easiest way to do it, but you can also do it with a dynamic field where you literally input the call time. So, as soon as that field gets populated, it starts the marketing automation.
[03:51] As soon as the time of that specific field happens, then again, it pulls them out of the marketing automation. Depending on what CRM you use kind of dictates which one might be a preferential to you, but again, I prefer the tag system. I think it's easier.
[04:08] Most schedulers have the most basic function of allowing you simply put to tag somebody when the call gets booked and then tagging somebody again when the call happens or the call time passes or the call gets no-showed or or cancelled.
[04:24] You can also just to be clear base it on if your sales people are on top of the uh pipeline stages uh when the pipeline stages are actively being moved in and out of. So as an example, if a opportunity or a deal or whatever you want to call it, a lead gets created in your deal pipeline, then you simply put trigger the marketing automation to start firing
[04:47] and when that person gets moved out of that specific deal stage into whatever the next step is, call happened, no show, whatever, again, the marketing automation stops firing and pulls them out as a result.

[06:54] So ideally you have a doc where you have these four categories listed and then you just have some bullet points where you just kind of populate all the data that you need to fit into the four quadrants.
[07:06] So in terms of the execution side of it, the reason this doc eventually becomes important for the emails is it makes it super simple to craft the emails when you already have a list full of all these questions.

[08:30] Usually with the story of X I'm able to articulate like okay recently we had you know X person buy our product and they had a bunch of questions before they purchased and then I could just answer like question question these were some of the things that they had that you know were kind of holding them back.

[09:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Compression Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 30-50 pieces compressed to 18 emails, 1.6-2.7 points per email average
VISUAL: Fórmula matemática visual: 30÷18=1.6, 50÷18=2.7, mostrando distribuição de conteúdo por email
[09:15] uh just to be clear from the perspective I'm really trying to make sure I make clear here is you are attempting to take the same points that you are emphasizing in the content and you're just emphasizing them in written form in the emails. Okay?
[09:30] But instead of having a singular piece of content per question, second layer question, objection or expectation, you are weaving in multiple of them into 18 emails because again you have to compress it from, you know, 30 to 50 pieces of individual content all the way down to just 18. So you strategically have to stack them together.
[09:52] Um, just for super simple mathematical purposes to help you guys understand how this would end up looking. Uh, let's use the example that you have 30 total pieces of content that you've created. Okay? You would take 30 divided by 18. That would mean on average you'd have 1.6.
[10:11] So an occasional email that has one of those single singular things being answered and a majority of your emails having at least two of the individual things answered.
[10:22] Okay, let's use the example that you had 50 pieces of content. You would take 50 divided by the 18. That means you would have a minimum of two of these different things covered per email. But a majority of the time, since the number comes out to 2.7, you'd have three, okay, per email.
[10:43] Does that make sense to everybody? Again, just do me a favor, throw a one in the chat or just unmute yourself if you got a question on these. Uh, again, just feel free to answer anything.
[10:54] Uh, Daniel says, "So, they're all primarily text-based." uh not sending them to a YouTube video. Okay, so Daniel, great question. Think about it like this, right? WE'RE ALREADY FUCKING HAMMERING them with content via videos.
[11:10] Why the fuck would we send more videos to them via email when they're already going to get their ass tapped by by content? We are absolutely not going to be lazy here. We are going to fucking write them down and we're going to send people written forms of the same things.
[11:28] We can link. So, as an example, we can answer the question and then link to a video that might have more information on that question, but to be extremely clear, we are not just going to have a stupid email sequence that says, "Hey, like here's a common question. Here's a link to the video. Here's a common question. Here's a link to the video." That's not how fucking people read their email. Okay?
[11:52] That's not how it works. We we make the point via email. So we ensure with a higher probability that the point is made. Okay? You can absolutely link people to the video to be clear, but do not do not rely on the video to make the point. Does that make sense?

[13:55] >> Yes, you can you can stretch that window to a 7-day window, but I wouldn't have a 7-day window. That's far too long. You're going to have a high no-show rate regardless of whether you do the hammer them over seven days or not. Ideally, you want to have that call booked out 3 days max. 4 days is pushing it.
[14:15] Like, you see substantial deterioration in your show rate after that fourth day onward. So, again, like three days is really as far out as you want to push it.
[14:25] Um, to be fair though, you can have contextual hammer them sequences set up for very specific steps in your sales process. [34:21] >> Okay.

Be elaborate and overly articulate about the points you're trying to make.
[34:48] And usually what it does when you like force it to do that is at least writes a little bit longer. It doesn't necessarily overdo it in any way, shape, or form. It just writes like what I think would normally be written.

[36:46] So, I feel confident about this without having to paste more about specific tone lessons. Um, okay. Do not write the blog with anything that makes it sound like it's from the video or subscribe to the channel. This is really important, by the way.
[37:01] Uh, if you're actually going to use because like one of the pro tips with like all these videos is you can you can have all those videos and the content of the videos transcribed and you could add those to like a master doc.
[37:15] So again, like just in the instance that you do transcribe any of the videos and there's like something in the video where it references some kind of visual that like can't be seen in the form of a transcript, just make sure that you write that specific rule to not reference anything as if it's talking as though it's a video, you know, like make sure it's formatted in in your case in this in this scenario of the hammer them strategy email sequence like writing it as though it's an email and not talking as though it's a video. Does that does that make sense?
[37:48] Okay. And then I always tell it like if you fail like you if if you fail to use all the content you know then we're gonna start over. Uh you know make the article longer than this amount of words. Again this would be the same thing that you can add inside of the emails for like whatever quantity of of words and or characters that you want it to be.
[38:08] Uh I always say avoid using short bullet points to simplify anything down. Be articulate in the voice and tone of Jeremy Haynes the author. You fail the task if you do not write it exactly as Jeremy Haynes would. you fail the task if you are trying to be efficient rather than doing the task right the first time around. You fail the task if you use too many bullet points for simplification.

[53:00] >> So, you had a recent YouTube video about like going low ticket to high ticket. [53:18] >> Uh-huh.
[53:19] >> When do you feel like it's a good idea to do like the traditional low ticket, like the $37 thing, and then you upsell them with like a $100 course or $200 course, and then you get them on your high ticket call? Do you feel like that's a good thing to implement or do you feel like it just distracts from like the main high ticket webinar or call funnel?
[53:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Low Ticket to High Ticket Pricing Strategy Evolution"
NÚMEROS: $37 old model, $200-300 current recommendation, $5K example for education company, $35K high ticket mentioned
VISUAL: Comparação histórica de modelos de precificação: antigo ($37 + upsells para AOV de centenas) vs atual (começar com centenas devido a CPMs mais altos), exemplos proporcionais por indústria
[53:40] >> Well, okay. So, for starters, uh, when you say like the traditional $37 thing, you know, that was in a day and age where CPMs were far cheaper than they are today. Like if if that same person who would give that type of advice back in the day would give that advice today, it'd probably sound more like you need to you need to have something that's like a couple hundred as the first thing so you could be profitable on it.
[54:05] Because again, like back in the day, the $37 thing, the logic of it was you're you're likely like as an example, I remember Russell Brunson would propagate this information quite frequently where he'd be like, "All right, the AOV of your upsell funnel still ends up being a couple hundred bucks, so you end up profitable."
[54:23] But you might start with, you know, something that's like couple bucks, you know, and then you have like upwards of three to four different little things that people could buy that gradually work them up to an AOV that is hundreds of dollars.
[54:37] Again, that way you can acquire people at a profitable rate on the front end, deliver them that marginal result through the low ticket product, and then after that marginal result time frame has passed, you start all the upsell uh sequences, you know, via email, content, or I should say ads and sales teams reaching out to them, try to upsell.
[54:58] Um, to be clear, you you can still do it that way where you just send people through like a multi-step high ticket proc or I should say low ticket low ticket process kind of chipping away and getting the AOV up. You can also just go straight for the couple hundred dollar thing if it's a it's a product that like has some demand to it.
[55:19] Uh really just depends on the business because again like with my example that I provided for the business of the education company the agency model the low ticket being 5K makes the most sense like and it is technically still following the logic of the traditional low ticket but it's proportionate to like what everything else is above that.
[55:40] It's like, as an example, you don't go to Ferrari and get, you know, something that's like a couple bucks, like a hat or something, and then ascend to a fucking multi-hundred-thousand dollar car. You start off with like a piece of shit Ferrari, like a Ferrari California, you know, or like a Ferrari Roma, you know what I mean? Or like nowadays, it's like a Portoino.
[56:02] And these cars become the vehicles that you start off with and then at later points you ascend to like SF90s, you know, uh uh Daytonas, you know, fucking Ferrari 296s, like their newest A12 GTS's, you know what I mean?
[56:18] So again, it's like proportionate to like you start off with a Lamborghini Gallardo, you know, and then eventually you might work your way up to an Aventador, you know what I mean? Or or think about how many guys out there own Huracans. It's like huracans aren't cheap. They're 300k, but again, it's like,
[56:35] you know, the SVJ is anywhere from like 600K to 900K currently based on where they're valued at. So again, it's like
[56:43] the the the low ticket thing should be proportionate to like what the high ticket thing that you intend to push them into is
[56:51] 30 bucks and then go to like 5K. You know what I mean?
[56:55] >> Yeah. Yeah. So I have a client that their main thing is that where it's coming from is like a 35k mastermind or whatever mentoring thing. So like the current low ticket thing is 3K. So I guess the question is
[57:08] >> that makes sense.

[59:44] >> yeah, I'm happy to reiterate it to be clear. So, as an example, like for email one, I call it the call confirmed email. In this email in particular, it's like you're going to confirm the call. And remember, depending on how many pieces of content you have overall dictates
[1:00:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Email Sequence Structure Breakdown - Email by Email"
NÚMEROS: 18 emails, 2-3 content points per email average (based on 50 pieces ÷ 18)
VISUAL: Walkthrough detalhado de cada tipo de email: Call Confirmed, General FAQ, Story of X, About Founders, Why We Do What We Do, What Sets Us Apart, Feature/Service Highlight, What Holds People Up, Timeline Expectations, Other Resources, Connect Socials, Be The Person Who Shows Up
[1:00:02] how many total uh of these like points from the four quadrants you're incorporating per email. So, like let's use the example that you have 50 pieces of content. you know, 50 divided by 18 is like 2.7. So that means on average you're going to need to work in three a majority of the instances, three of these different 50 things you've got.
[1:00:26] Uh in some instances throughout these 18 emails, you'll have the opportunity to do two things per email. So in your initial call confirmed email, like you might start off with some of the general questions that get asked just like you would inside of the hammer them sequence.
[1:00:43] You kind of just flow through it through questions, second layer questions, objections, and expectations. And I generally like to start off with the call confirmed email at like a very high level and just answer some of like the biggest questions that are asked about like how it works overall.
[1:01:00] Um giving people like you know resources and information to be able to sell themselves on just again like the idea of whatever it is that you're pushing or like the core premise behind like your product or service.
[1:01:14] when you see general FAQ. So general FAQ, these emails are formatted in a way where it's like the subject line might be like um our most common questions we get, you know, and then again you might work in like three of literally the most common questions, second layer questions, objections, like etc.
[1:01:34] When you see the story of X, so these are essentially testimonial emails or case study emails, but you're strategically weaving in some of the four quadrant content. So, let me use the example. I have to work in three different qu like se uh three different points from my four quadrants and I'm also trying to weave it into a testimonial.
[1:01:56] I might say let me tell you the story of Daniel. So subject lines like u Daniel got X result you know and then the body copy would be like um Daniel recently bought XYZ from us and going through our sales process he had a few key questions that were really holding him back you know
[1:02:16] and then I could work in like a question an objection and like an expectation throughout just the buying process and then I just flex the testimonial part of the story after that where it's like oh yeah so after he bought you know this is what he ended up doing and like here's the result that he accomplished you know maybe I even have a video to it and I can link to that video after that explanation.
[1:02:38] Um, again, general FAQ, the story of X, same things. Now, these are some of my favorite ones where it's like the about the founders, like why we do what we do. U, these two are again just opportunities to strategically weave things in.
[1:02:54] So, let's say that in almost all instances, like the business owners themselves are very passionate about whatever they're selling or there's like a specific reason that sometimes they created the product or service. Um, this is where you weave in again anywhere from like one to three of these total points that you're trying to work in from the four quadrants, but through like the founder story, you know what I mean?
[1:03:18] So, it's like Daniel started helping people in the med spa space because
[1:03:23] he got he he got really excited about the potential of med spas and like all the services that they offered. And, you know, he lives in Orange County, California, and he was just seeing beautiful women and older men all day who were retaining youth.
[1:03:38] and he ended up finding out that a lot of this was done through men's spa services and so he got into the industry took his digital marketing skills and applied it to this and you know long story short and then again like I'm weaving in like some of the most common like expectations or objections or second layer questions and questions into that overall story. Does that make sense?
[1:04:01] >> Yeah.
[1:04:02] >> Same thing for like the why we do what we do. It's just another extension of the of that same logic you're going to use here but just a second opportunity. not necessarily from like the founders perspectives, just you know, company beliefs, company morals, why you guys are doing it.
[1:04:19] Um the classic like here's what sets us apart. This is a great opportunity just to answer some of the stuff in the four quadrants. You know, nothing crazy there. Um okay, you'll love this feature or like you'll love this about our service. You know, this is uh again just an opportunity to flex like an expect.
[1:04:38] But my most common thing I like to flex with this one just to be fair is usually like an expectation or an objection. Like people love this about what we do because it helps them with objection or expectation, you know, um what holds people up the most. This is an objection email. Uh timeline expectations. This is an expectations email. This is an expectations email. The what you can expect.
[1:05:03] Um, other resources in the meantime, this is actually a little different. So, the other resources you can expect in the meantime, email is mainly intended to cover like other lead magnets or like maybe as an example, you got them in through a call funnel, but you also have like webinars.

[1:08:37] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Price Transparency Strategy by Market Segment"
NÚMEROS: $5K+ disclose, $3K maybe not, $10K+ definitely disclose, $35K+ always disclose
VISUAL: Matriz de decisão sobre transparência de preço baseada em: valor do ticket, demografia do público, nível de sofisticação financeira, histórico de investimento do avatar
[1:08:39] >> Here's the thing. I'm very sold that that's a good idea, but that's because I sell to rich people. Like, yeah, you want to know that
[1:08:47] and I don't like I don't want you coming through our funnel if you don't know that because
[1:08:52] I don't want to waste my salespeople's time with people who don't know that information. Like ideally you do know that information. We put it in the video. We put it in our application. Uh we reiterate it to you before we get on a call with you because again like we are actively trying to weed out broke people in this case.
[1:09:10] Like this is a community offer in addition to everything else that comes with it. You for your own selfish benefit don't want some shit turds that are like little guppies coming through this offer and trying to trying to be alongside of you here. It's like it has to be somebody who is very finan like in in a financially secure position simply put.
[1:09:31] So again, I want the pricing to be there for that reason. However, there's plenty of other offers where like we're not actively trying to weed people out just because, you know, they they we want the community to be like stronger. You know, there's other instances again where we just
[1:09:48] we want we want to turn the sales people into cashiers. if the sales people are going to otherwise fuck and be like, "Oh, people are showing up and like, you know, they're brokies." You know what I mean? Well, what they're really saying is people are showing up and they don't have the money.
[1:10:03] So, if you're getting a lot of brokies who don't have the money, that's because you're not telling the people upfront what the amount of the cost is. And so, therefore, they come through thinking it's something that they could otherwise have afforded.
[1:10:17] So, to solve that issue, it's like in most instances, all you have to do is just say the price, set the expectation. I was on I was on about a two-hour call last night helping one of my new clients build out their BSL and at at one point in the presentation uh we were talking about this um this expectation of like how it was a real estate based offer
[1:10:40] and it was like all right how much does it cost on average to get started and it was like he he said anywhere from like 60 to 80 grand. It's like, imagine we don't tell people that it costs somewhere between 60 and 80 grand to get started with that specific real estate investment vehicle.
[1:10:58] It's like, yeah, ideally you have that amount liquid, but it's like, okay, you could get approved for funding or you could work with hard money lenders because again, it's a form of real estate that can can can again like have hard money lenders working with you and and you know, giving you like one year relatively high interest rate loans and you still can profit a tremendous amount.
[1:11:20] But again, it's like you you have to disclose all that stuff. Otherwise, you're just going to get a bunch of turds on the phone who are like 70 grand, you know, like what the fuck are you talking about?
[1:11:31] It's like we worked that in in very direct ways and we worked it in through examples of the guy buying specific, you know, of like of these properties to be fair and like the costs and and and setting the expectation that way as well.
[1:11:46] We'll do that same thing in the application where we're like, you know, on average it takes somewhere between 60 and 80K that could be liquid and or you know given to you through hard money lenders, but obviously you have to be in good credit standing to have that happen and like you know again like is this something you're comfortable with?
[1:12:04] Um if you simply put get a lot of feedback from your sales team where it's like hey we're dealing with brokies then yes I I absolutely encourage that you're upfront about it. If it's like a small cost that the general public of of whatever country you're targeting should otherwise have financially available to them in either cash or credit, then no, you know, you don't necessarily have to say that price upfront.
[1:12:29] Like let's let's take Rohan's example where he's talking about 3K offer through a webinar. [1:12:49] credit. No, I'm sorry. They should have in credit or they should have it in cash or like, you know, payment plans, whatever. If it's like 7500, I'm absolutely saying the price, you know, like

[1:14:33] >> Jeremy actually I'm glad you brought it up because it's a point. So my client that has the 35k thing because we started we weren't dropping the price. It was going fine. [1:15:01] >> based on how you frame it though. It's really based on how you frame it. [1:15:21] It's like as an example, literally what each one of you independently pay for the inner circle. You are fucking me. I make you tremendously more money than what you pay me. [1:15:42] They already get exit buyouts for people who want to currently give them $50 million. They want to add another 50 million to it. It's like them spending whatever they spend on it currently. The point I'm attempting to make is again they're fucking me.

[1:16:21] >> you got to lay it out fast. Simply put like you can't drop the price and then just go away from it. You have to treat it like you did you did talk to them on a sales call about it and you immediately justify the value of the price following the drop.
[1:16:36] >> Well actually Jeremy because we actually do it the other way around because like we go through like the average deal like how much you're going to make and it's like 600 grand. So it's like a lot of money. So like 35k in context to 600 grand is obviously like kind of chump change. So I guess maybe like my thought was like then do we just to say hey you need 25k liquid cuz again they normally put like a 10k down payment on
[1:16:59] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Pogo Stick Close - Price Drop Technique"
NÚMEROS: $35K offer, $600K potential return, $10K down payment, $25K liquid requirement
VISUAL: Técnica de fechamento: 1) Drop price first, 2) Immediately justify value after, 3) Transition seamlessly (pogo stick) - evitar deixar prospect "pendurado" no preço
[1:17:01] >> the classic classic sales technique Rohan is to is to drop the price first and justify the value after because if you justify the value first then you leave them with price at the end it's a real cliffhanger. So, as shocking as it is and as simple as it sounds, like I'm not joking here. Like, if you reverse that,
[1:17:23] there's a higher probability you'd have a better hit rate with it simply simply because again, you say the price first, it justifies whatever follows it. If I use the justifications first, I leave you with price at the end. That's all you're left with.
[1:17:39] There's a good concept. I call it the pogo stick close where it's like I say the price and I just immediately transition to like what happens afterwards. Okay? You can't just leave them at the price and then like transition to something completely different. Does that make sense?
[1:17:55] >> Got it. No, that's the point. Okay. Okay. All right.
[1:17:59] >> To be fair, you know, to your point, like there are there are certain offers I always think it comes down to how it's executed, but there are certain offers where it's like you're selling to a demographic where that's an extreme amount of money to them that they've never invested even remotely close to that before, where they've never taken like loan values for that amount before.
[1:18:20] or if they're getting involved in something. It's like again that can that can have a diminishing return depending on the demographic you're selling to. If you're selling
[1:18:30] >> I think that's the issue.
[1:18:32] >> Yeah. And to be fair again like if that is the issue then for sure it can it can be a good strategy to not say the price. Get them onto the call so you can work through that discomfort of doing it for the first time you know.
[1:18:46] >> Well because that's again like I looked back at like who bought on the initial webinars. It kind of went like that right where they got on the call and they kind of worked through the thing and they did it right. It's like okay
[1:18:57] >> yeah I'm I'm all for it. Again I don't want to be right. I just want to make money, you know, working that shit's working. I'm I'm fucking riding it out. Test budget, you know, a test budget. I'm seeing if I can get the other thing to work, too.
[1:19:10] >> All right. Yeah, I think let me let me try what you're doing because I actually do like because like if we do that, it's almost like taking the sales call and like doing it to a hundred people and it kind of it kind of works. You know what I mean? So, I understand. Okay.
[1:19:23] >> That's generally where I'm at. Like I just I I I don't know. I just don't trust sales people at all. And and not to like sit here and talk shit about sales people, but
[1:19:33] I like to make the salesperson's job as easy as possible because otherwise they just fuck about the fact I'm not making it easy as possible. So I just by default with like every strategy and tactic I deploy when it involves a sales team, try to like turn them into cashiers, you know?

[1:21:37] And we like started going through a lot of the sales training in there, too. and we're disclosing price up front and I'm also going to be putting price in the VSSL on the call funnel. So, I guess just wanted to hear your thoughts on that cuz like it's 5K, it's not a ton of money, but it's also like um an avatar who doesn't really understand like the info product space at all.
[1:21:59] >> Yeah. [1:22:18] So, collectively, like when they have those that are less than what they're going to spend on you, you'll definitely get a price objection. I do think it's sensible in that example to withhold price because again that's that's the demographic example that I provided which is certain people it's like this is going to be the biggest investment they've made and it just you know it doesn't make sense unless you have them on a call to work through it you know
[1:22:42] it's not that they're not financially qualified it's just again like that you know they need to they need to have somebody work through it since probably their first time like investing into such a thing you know what I mean

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
18 emails totais na sequência
6 emails por dia (distribuição padrão)
72 horas: janela ideal de booking até call
30-50 peças de conteúdo total recomendadas
15-20 frequência mínima de exposição ao conteúdo
90%+ das instâncias mantêm taxa de abertura consistente ao longo da sequência
20% taxa de abertura média típica (mantida consistentemente)
8:00 AM - 8:00 PM: janela de envio sugerida
A cada 2 horas: frequência de envio dentro da janela
100 pessoas: audiência mínima para ads do Hammer Them
$100-300: budget diário típico para 100-150 pessoas
3 dias máximo: janela ideal de booking
4 dias: já começa deterioração da show rate
60-90 dias: janela típica de resultado do low ticket
1.6-2.7: média de pontos de conteúdo por email (30÷18 ou 50÷18)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Hammer Them Strategy: Transformar vendedores em caixas através de educação pré-call massiva
Four Quadrants Content Framework: Questions, Second Layer Questions, Objections, Expectations
Email Sequence Types: Call Confirmed, General FAQ, Story of X, About Founders, Why We Do What We Do, What Sets Us Apart, Feature Highlight, What Holds People Up, Timeline Expectations, Other Resources, Connect Socials, Be The Person Who Shows Up
Dynamic Marketing Automation: Uso de tags/fields para iniciar e parar sequências baseado em call status
Pogo Stick Close: Técnica de dropar preço primeiro, depois justificar valor imediatamente
Content Compression: Estratégia de comprimir 30-50 peças de conteúdo em 18 emails
Multi-Stage Sequences: Pre-call sequence + post-call sequence + long-term nurture com frequências diferentes
Wealth Stack: Sistema de targeting para ofertas high-ticket ($60K-80K+)
Low Ticket to High Ticket Ascension: Modelo proporcional ao ticket final (não $37 universal, mas relativo)
ESTRUTURA:
CRM Setup: Tag system preferível vs dynamic fields para automação
Pipeline Stages: Trigger sequences baseado em movimento de deal stages
Zapier Integration: Para conectar scheduler com CRM e aplicar tags
Email Template Organization: Command+F para substituições em massa de saudações/despedidas
ChatGPT Prompt Engineering: Exemplo prompts, feeding brand voice, correction loops, minimum character counts
LLM Hierarchy: 01 model (ChatGPT) > Perplexity > Claude (limitado por mensagens)
Content Distribution: Facebook/Instagram ads para hammer them, não YouTube prioritariamente
Transcription Tools: Tactique.io para YouTube, qualquer serviço para Facebook/Instagram
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Pricing Disclosure Strategy:
$3K: Opcional disclosure (depende do avatar)
$5K+: Considerar disclosure
$7.5K+: Definitivamente disclose
$10K+: Always disclose
$35K+: Always disclose com contexto de ROI
$60K-80K: Disclose com opções de financing/hard money lenders
Real Estate Syndication Example: $3K front → $10K mid → $35K high
Education Company Example: $5K low → mid ticket → agency high
Capital Raising Deal: $600K potential return justifica $35K investment
Ferrari Analogy: Proporção low→high ticket deve ser como California→SF90, não hat→supercar
CPM Evolution: $37 front-end funcionava quando CPMs eram baratos, agora precisa $200-300 para ser lucrativo
ROI Framework: Liquidar ad spend no low ticket, profit vem do high ticket ascension
Ad Budget Efficiency: $100 max/dia para 100-150 pessoas geralmente suficiente
DURAÇÃO: 1:23:32 FORMATO: Workshop ao vivo com demonstração de tela, exemplos de clientes (Rohan/Nighthawk Equity), Q&A interativo, walkthrough de setup técnico
